name: Cloudflare Radar API
what_it_does: Free internet intelligence API from Cloudflare providing global traffic trends, BGP routing data, DNS query statistics, attack trends, and internet quality metrics aggregated from Cloudflare's global network.
use_cases: Monitoring global internet traffic patterns and anomalies for situational awareness
Detecting DDoS attack trends by country, industry, or protocol
Researching DNS query patterns and emerging malicious domains
Analyzing internet quality metrics (latency, bandwidth) by region
BGP route leak and hijack detection for network security monitoring
not_for: Per-IP reputation or individual threat lookups (use AbuseIPDB or VirusTotal)
Real-time traffic blocking or WAF integration
Detailed logs for your own traffic (use Cloudflare Workers analytics instead)
Non-internet-scale trend data — this is aggregate, not per-entity
best_when: An agent needs macro-level internet intelligence: attack trends, traffic shifts, or DNS patterns to inform security decisions or situational awareness reports.
avoid_when: You need individual IP/domain verdicts or your own traffic data.

auth.notes: Uses Cloudflare API tokens with specific permission scopes. Bearer token via Authorization header. No OAuth flow needed — token-based only. Many Radar endpoints are publicly accessible without authentication.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Most Radar API endpoints are completely free with no hard limits
pricing.paid_tiers: Part of Cloudflare ecosystem — paid plans unlock other Cloudflare products
pricing.notes: Radar data is Cloudflare's contribution to internet transparency. Most endpoints require no authentication or billing. Extremely generous for research and agent use.

performance.rate_limits: Generous; specific limits not publicly documented

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Standard Cloudflare API error format with 'errors' array. Error messages are generally clear but documentation on specific error codes is sparse.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: All Radar endpoints are read-only GET requests — fully idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: Data represents aggregate trends, not individual entity verdicts — easy to misinterpret
Time range parameters vary by endpoint — some use date strings, others use offsets
Some endpoints return data with 15-minute to 1-hour lag — not truly real-time
BGP and routing data has complex schema that requires domain knowledge to interpret correctly
Undocumented rate limits may apply — no clear published thresholds
